c.name: The Kōrōden
r.name: The Kōrōden
e.evidence: At her palace chambers, with the Emperor at her side, the dying Consort "still managed to compose a verse for him" (Washburn NC pp. 5–6). Those chambers are the Kōrōden — she had been "installed in the chambers of the Kōrōden, a hall next to the Seiryōden" (Washburn NC p. 5).
d.evidence: Received in person by the Emperor, present at the bedside: he "returned to her chambers again" and "could not bring himself to let her go" when she composed the verse (Washburn NC pp. 5–6).

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
e.evidence: The Emperor "had even attached a poem" to the letter he gave Myōbu before sending her out (Washburn NC pp. 7–8). Composition location inferred from context — the hall is not named here; the Emperor's quarters are the Seiryōden (Washburn NC pp. 4, 12).
d.evidence: Carried by Myōbu and delivered at the grandmother's home, where she "looked at the note" to which the Emperor "had even attached a poem" (Washburn NC p. 8).

c.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
r.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
e.evidence: Uttered at the grandmother's home as Myōbu prepares to leave at dawn: she "found it hard to leave this desolate abode," and the verse follows (Washburn NC p. 10).
d.evidence: The grandmother is present at her home and at once "sent a response through an intermediary" (Washburn NC p. 10); co-present.

c.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
r.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
e.evidence: The grandmother "sent a response through an intermediary" to Myōbu, composed within her home (Washburn NC p. 10).
d.evidence: Received by Myōbu, still at the house — "Myōbu was reluctant to board her carriage" (Washburn NC p. 10); same location.

c.name: Home of Genji's Grandmother
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: The grandmother's "written response to his request," carried back by Myōbu with the memento robes (Washburn NC pp. 10–11). Composition location inferred from context — the writing is not depicted; she is at her home throughout the visit (Washburn NC pp. 7–10).
d.evidence: Read by the Emperor at the palace: Myōbu "returned to the palace" and found him "gazing westward into the garden between the Seiryōden and the Kōrōden" (Washburn NC p. 10), where "he read the grandmother's written response" (Washburn NC p. 11).

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: A soliloquy in the same palace scene, as Myōbu "had the mementos brought forth" and the Emperor recalls Xuanzong's Taoist summoner (Washburn NC p. 11). He is in his quarters, "gazing westward into the garden between the Seiryōden and the Kōrōden" (Washburn NC p. 10).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly); same location.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: A soliloquy composed at night; the place is named — "his private chambers in the Seiryōden" (Washburn NC p. 12) — and "The moon was waning" immediately precedes the verse (Washburn NC p. 12).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly); same location.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: Recited in person at Genji's coming-of-age: "His Majesty was seated . . . under the eastern eaves of his residence in the Seiryōden" (Washburn NC p. 16), and "presenting a cup of rice wine to the Minister" he recites the verse (Washburn NC p. 17).
d.evidence: The Minister of the Left, summoned to the imperial quarters in the Seiryōden, is present and replies on the spot (Washburn NC pp. 17–18); co-present.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: "The Minister composed this reply" at the ceremony, immediately after the Emperor's verse and before he "stepped down from the long bridge . . . and performed obeisance in the east garden" (Washburn NC p. 18) — within the Seiryōden ceremony space.
d.evidence: Received in person by the Emperor, present at the Seiryōden; co-present, same location.

c.name: House of the Finger Biting Lady
r.name: House of the Finger Biting Lady
e.evidence: Within the Warden's anecdote, after the quarrel in which she "grabbed my hand and bit one of my fingers," he "composed a poem" in her presence (Washburn NC p. 29).
d.evidence: Heard in person by the woman at the house of the Finger-Biting Lady, who at once "burst into tears and shot back" her reply (Washburn NC p. 29); co-present.

c.name: House of the Finger Biting Lady
r.name: House of the Finger Biting Lady
e.evidence: Her reply within the same anecdote, in person: she "burst into tears and shot back" the verse (Washburn NC p. 29).
d.evidence: Heard by the Warden, present at the house of the Finger-Biting Lady; co-present, same dwelling.

c.name: Home of the Warden's Lady
r.name: Home of the Warden's Lady
e.evidence: Within the Warden's second anecdote, on the veranda of the woman's house the courtier "picked some chrysanthemums and composed a poem" (Washburn NC p. 31). Her house is described — garden, pond, bamboo blinds, an "open veranda near the inner gate" (Washburn NC p. 31).
d.evidence: The woman, inside behind the blinds at the Warden's lady's house, "replied to his brazen bantering" (Washburn NC pp. 31–32); co-present.

c.name: Home of the Warden's Lady
r.name: Home of the Warden's Lady
e.evidence: Her reply within the anecdote, composed inside her house: "The woman replied to his brazen bantering, affecting a disinterested voice" (Washburn NC p. 32).
d.evidence: Heard by the courtier on her veranda at the Warden's lady's house; co-present.

c.name: Yūgao's Original House
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Within Tō no Chūjō's anecdote, the woman at her home "sent me a wild pink . . . She sent this poem" (Washburn NC p. 33); composed at her "dilapidated house" (Washburn NC p. 33).
d.evidence: Sent as a message to Tō no Chūjō; Washburn does not state where he received it. Location inferred from context — he keeps "dazzlingly furnished quarters at his father's residence at Sanjō" (the Minister of the Left's; Washburn NC p. 20), the likelier place for a private note since the threats against her came from "my wife's household" (Washburn NC p. 33).

c.name: Yūgao's Original House
r.name: Yūgao's Original House
e.evidence: Within the anecdote, Tō no Chūjō visits her: "With her verse fresh in my mind, I went to see her," and at her house composes the verse (Washburn NC p. 33).
d.evidence: Heard by the woman, present at her house; co-present.

c.name: Yūgao's Original House
r.name: Yūgao's Original House
e.evidence: Her reply at her house within the anecdote: "She replied with this" (Washburn NC p. 33).
d.evidence: Heard by Tō no Chūjō, present; co-present at her house.

c.name: The Chinese Scholar's Daughter's House
r.name: The Chinese Scholar's Daughter's House
e.evidence: Within the Junior Secretary's anecdote, leaving the learned woman's house because of the garlic smell, he "sent this poem" (Washburn NC pp. 35–36). She is a daughter of the scholar at whose academy he studied (Washburn NC p. 35).
d.evidence: Received by the woman, present behind a screen at her house (Washburn NC pp. 35–36); co-present.

c.name: The Chinese Scholar's Daughter's House
r.name: The Chinese Scholar's Daughter's House
e.evidence: Her quick reply at her house within the anecdote: "her reply came chasing me down" (Washburn NC p. 36).
d.evidence: Received by the Secretary as he left her house; co-present.

c.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: At dawn after their night, in the interior chamber of the Governor of Kii's mansion: "As the cock crowed again and again . . . He composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 43).
d.evidence: Heard by Utsusemi at the Governor of Kii's mansion, whom Genji "detained . . . for a moment" at parting; co-present (Washburn NC p. 43).

c.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: Utsusemi's dawn reply, composed in person: "She replied" (Washburn NC p. 43).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Governor of Kii's mansion; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: Composed days later at his father-in-law's house: "Genji began to spend all of his time at his father-in-law's residence" (Washburn NC p. 44) — the Minister of the Left's, at Sanjō — where he "had written a great deal" (Washburn NC pp. 44–45).
d.evidence: Delivered by the boy Kogimi: "when he delivered Genji's letter, he was stunned to see tears welling up in his sister's eyes" (Washburn NC p. 44). Location inferred from context — Washburn does not name where Utsusemi is; she had been lodging at the Governor of Kii's mansion during her household's purification (Washburn NC p. 39), where Genji's next visit again finds her (Washburn NC pp. 46–47).

c.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: On a second directional-taboo visit — Genji "dropped in on the Governor of Kii" (Washburn NC p. 46) — after Utsusemi eludes him he writes from the guest quarters: "Is there no way to tell you how I feel?" he wrote (Washburn NC p. 47).
d.evidence: Carried within the residence to Utsusemi, hiding "in the quarters of her chief attendant, Chūjō . . . a private part of the residence" (Washburn NC p. 46); same mansion.

c.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: Utsusemi, "unable to sleep," replies from her hiding place in Chūjō's quarters at the Kii mansion (Washburn NC p. 47).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji in the guest quarters of the same residence; same mansion.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
e.evidence: After the failed night Genji "returned to his villa at Nijō" (Washburn NC p. 53); unable to sleep, "He demanded an inkstone be brought at once, and . . . composed a poem" on folded paper (Washburn NC p. 54).
d.evidence: Delivered by the boy Kogimi: "The boy took the poem . . . and delivered it to his sister"; "When the boy arrived at his sister's residence, she was waiting for him" (Washburn NC p. 54). Location inferred from context — the residence is not named here; it is the Governor of Kii's mansion, where Utsusemi is lodging (Washburn NC pp. 48–49).

c.name: Governor of Kii's Mansion
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: Recalling Genji, Utsusemi "wrote out a poem along the edge of Genji's letter to her" (Washburn NC p. 55). Composition location inferred from context — her whereabouts are not named here; she is at the Governor of Kii's mansion, where she received his letter (Washburn NC pp. 54–55).
d.evidence: Washburn gives no indication the poem was sent to or received by Genji: she writes it on the edge of his own letter and the narrative moves on (Washburn NC p. 55). Receipt cannot be determined.

c.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
r.name: Koremitsu's Mother's House on Gojō
e.evidence: The lady's verse is written on a scented fan sent out from the house next to Genji's nurse's — her own Gojō house: a girl "handed him a heavily perfumed white fan" bearing the verse (Washburn NC pp. 56–57).
d.evidence: Genji reads it at his nurse's house, having a "small wooden hand torch" brought "so that he could examine the fan" (Washburn NC p. 57).

c.name: Koremitsu's Mother's House on Gojō
r.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
e.evidence: Still at his nurse's house, Genji "pulled out a piece of folded paper . . . set down his reply" and handed it to the guard (Washburn NC p. 58).
d.evidence: Carried to the lady's house, where her attendants debated until the guard "went back to Genji empty-handed" (Washburn NC p. 58).

c.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
r.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
e.evidence: One foggy morning leaving Rokujō, Genji seats the lady-in-waiting on the veranda railing: "He took her hand and composed the following" (Washburn NC p. 61).
d.evidence: Spoken to the lady-in-waiting, present, who "skillfully parried his poem" on the spot (Washburn NC p. 61).

c.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
r.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
e.evidence: Her reply in person at Rokujō: she "skillfully parried his poem by taking it to refer to her mistress" (Washburn NC p. 61).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present; co-present at Lady Rokujō's mansion.

c.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
r.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
e.evidence: At dawn on the Harvest-Moon night, "Moonlight streamed in . . . illuminating every corner of the woman's residence" (Washburn NC p. 65); moved by a pilgrim's prayers, "Powerful emotions prompted him to compose a verse" (Washburn NC p. 66).
d.evidence: Spoken to Yūgao, present; co-present at her Gojō house (Washburn NC p. 66).

c.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
r.name: Yūgao's House on Gojō
e.evidence: Her doubtful reply at her residence: she "thought that future vows were too extravagant" (Washburn NC p. 66).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Yūgao's house on Gojō; co-present.

c.name: An Untenanted Mansion
r.name: An Untenanted Mansion
e.evidence: At the deserted riverside villa, gazing at the ferns under the weathered gate, "His feeling prompted the following" (Washburn NC p. 67).
d.evidence: Spoken to Yūgao, present at the untenanted mansion; co-present at the villa (Washburn NC p. 67).

c.name: An Untenanted Mansion
r.name: An Untenanted Mansion
e.evidence: Her reply at the villa: "The woman blushed, then replied" (Washburn NC p. 67).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the untenanted mansion; co-present.

c.name: An Untenanted Mansion
r.name: An Untenanted Mansion
e.evidence: Later, uncovering his face for her, Genji "composed this verse" at the villa (Washburn NC p. 68).
d.evidence: Spoken to Yūgao, present at the untenanted mansion; co-present at the villa.

c.name: An Untenanted Mansion
r.name: An Untenanted Mansion
e.evidence: Her reply at the villa: she "whispered" the verse (Washburn NC p. 68).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the untenanted mansion; co-present. Yūgao dies here that night.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: A month after Yūgao's death "he left for his quarters at the palace" (Washburn NC p. 77); one evening, as "the plants in the front garden of the palace were withering," he speaks the verse to Ukon — "He spoke in a barely audible whisper" (Washburn NC p. 80).
d.evidence: Heard by Ukon, present and serving him, though she "was unable to compose a reply" (Washburn NC p. 80).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: Fearing Genji had forgotten her before her departure for the provinces, Utsusemi "sent a letter" (Washburn NC p. 80). Her location is not stated.
d.evidence: Brought to Genji at the palace, where his page "would come to the palace" (Washburn NC p. 80); "Genji was surprised by this rare message" (Washburn NC p. 81).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: Genji, at the palace, "sent a response" to her letter, his hand still trembling from illness (Washburn NC p. 81).
d.evidence: Received by Utsusemi — "she found it delightful and poignant that he had not forgotten her" (Washburn NC p. 81) — but her location is not stated.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Nokiba no Ogi's marital home
e.evidence: Genji, at the palace, "sent her a message as well," attaching the verse to a tall reed and telling his page "to deliver it in secret" (Washburn NC p. 81).
d.evidence: "The page showed the stepdaughter the letter when her husband was out" (Washburn NC p. 81) — at her marital home (the Lesser Captain's residence), unnamed.

c.name: Nokiba no Ogi's marital home
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: She "quickly gave the boy her reply — a rather sloppy letter . . . written in haste" at her marital home (Washburn NC p. 81).
d.evidence: Carried by the page to Genji at the palace (Washburn NC pp. 81–82).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: For the 49th-day rites Genji "secretly ordered a pair of trousers to be made in her name and brought in as an offering," composing the verse over them (Washburn NC p. 82). Composition location inferred from context — the rites are at the Lotus Hall on Mt Hiei (Washburn NC p. 82), but the text does not place Genji there; this private verse falls in his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo, Washburn NC p. 77).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly); same location, inferred from context.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: As the Iyo Deputy leaves the capital, Genji "returned the robe he had taken from her that night with this poem attached" (Washburn NC p. 83). Composition location inferred from context — no residence is named here; Genji is in his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo, Washburn NC pp. 77, 80).
d.evidence: Sent by messenger to Utsusemi as her household departs the capital; her whereabouts are not stated (Washburn NC p. 83).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: Utsusemi "sent nothing back with Genji's messenger, but had her younger brother . . . convey her reply" (Washburn NC p. 83); her departing whereabouts are not stated.
d.evidence: Carried by her brother (the page) to Genji (Washburn NC p. 83). Receipt location inferred from context — no residence is named here; Genji is in his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo, Washburn NC pp. 77, 80).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: At the start of winter — "Today marked the start of winter . . . he spent the time staring out in dark contemplation" — Genji composes the verse contrasting the dead Yūgao and the departed Utsusemi (Washburn NC p. 83). Location inferred from context — no setting is named here; his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo, Washburn NC pp. 77, 80).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly); same location, inferred from context.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: As death nears, the nun grieves over the orphaned girl and "composed a verse" in her own quarters at the bishop's residence (Washburn NC p. 88).
d.evidence: Addressed to her attendant, present in the room, who weeps and replies at once (Washburn NC pp. 88–89).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: At the Bishop of Kitayama’s mountain residence, in the nun’s quarters, the attendant answers the nun in person — she “composed this reply” (Washburn NC pp. 88–89); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by the nun, present at the Bishop of Kitayama's residence; co-present in her quarters.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: Hosted in the south-facing guest room of the bishop's residence, Genji taps a fan at the screen and gives the verse to an attendant to relay (Washburn NC p. 92).
d.evidence: Carried by the attendant to the nun within the same residence; she puzzles over it before replying (Washburn NC p. 92).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: At the Bishop’s mountain residence, the nun, in her own quarters, “sent the following” to Genji through the attendant (Washburn NC p. 92).
d.evidence: Relayed to Genji in his guest room; the nun soon moves nearer to speak with him in person (Washburn NC pp. 92–93).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: At dawn, hearing the monks' confessions and the waterfall, Genji "sent a verse to the bishop" from within the residence (Washburn NC p. 93).
d.evidence: Received by the bishop, who "replied" (Washburn NC p. 93).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: The bishop's reply, composed at his residence (Washburn NC pp. 93–94).
d.evidence: Received by Genji, present at the residence.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: At his departure, accepting rice wine, Genji recites the verse in person at the residence (Washburn NC p. 94).
d.evidence: Heard by the bishop, present; co-present.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: The bishop "replied" in person at the parting (Washburn NC p. 94).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Bishop of Kitayama's residence; co-present.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: The old healer, who "managed to make his way to the bishop's residence," receives the winecup and recites the verse in tearful reverence (Washburn NC pp. 94–95).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Bishop of Kitayama's residence, present; co-present.

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: About to board his carriage, Genji has "one of the pages serving at the bishop's residence take a note to her" (Washburn NC p. 95).
d.evidence: Carried by the page to the nun within the residence; she "dashed off her reply" (Washburn NC p. 95).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: The nun's reply, written at her quarters in an elegant, graceful hand (Washburn NC p. 95).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji at the residence as he departs.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: "The day after returning from his mountain retreat, Genji sent letters to the house in Kitayama" (Washburn NC p. 98). Composition location inferred from context — he had gone to "spend a leisurely day or two at" the Minister of the Left's residence (Washburn NC p. 97), which the text does not re-name at the moment of writing.
d.evidence: Sent "to the house in Kitayama" — the bishop's residence, where Murasaki and the nun are (Washburn NC p. 98).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: "The nun sent a reply" from her quarters at the bishop's residence in Kitayama (Washburn NC p. 98).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji. Receipt location inferred from context — he is staying at the Minister of the Left's residence the day or two after returning (Washburn NC p. 97); the residence is not re-named here.

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
e.evidence: "After a few days he sent Koremitsu off" to Kitayama with a further letter, again enclosing a folded note (Washburn NC p. 98). Composition location not stated — around this time Genji "idled away the days at the palace or at his own villa" (Washburn NC p. 99).
d.evidence: Carried by Koremitsu to the bishop's residence, where Murasaki and the nun are (Washburn NC pp. 98–99).

c.name: Home of the Bishop of Kitayama
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: Murasaki's reply, written for her at the bishop's residence by her grandmother — "The nun replied for the girl" (Washburn NC p. 99).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji via Koremitsu; his capital residence is not stated (Washburn NC p. 99).

c.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
r.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
e.evidence: During the tryst Ōmyōbu arranges at the home where the ailing Fujitsubo has withdrawn, Genji — sobbing — recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 99–100).
d.evidence: Heard by Fujitsubo, present; co-present. Afterward "Ōmyōbu gathered up his robes" and Genji left (Washburn NC p. 100).

c.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
r.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
e.evidence: "Feeling pity for him, she replied" in person during the tryst (Washburn NC p. 100).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Fujitsubo's residence; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Late Major Counselor's residence
e.evidence: The day after visiting the ailing nun — "he made his way home" (Washburn NC p. 102) — "The next day Genji sent a most solicitous letter . . . he included a small folded letter for the girl" (Washburn NC p. 103). Composition location inferred from context — "home" is his Nijō villa; no residence is named at the moment of writing.
d.evidence: Sent to the late Major Counselor's residence, where the nun and the girl are; Shōnagon replies (Washburn NC p. 103).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: "One autumn evening," preoccupied with longing for Fujitsubo, Genji thinks of the girl and an image of wakamurasaki "popped into his head" (Washburn NC p. 103). No setting is given for this private verse.
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Late Major Counselor's residence
r.name: The Late Major Counselor's residence
e.evidence: Visiting the late nun's residence one evening, received in the same south-side space as before, Genji recites the verse to Shōnagon (Washburn NC pp. 103–104).
d.evidence: Heard by Shōnagon, present at the late Major Counselor's residence; co-present.

c.name: The Late Major Counselor's residence
r.name: The Late Major Counselor's residence
e.evidence: Shōnagon's reply, spoken in person at the residence (Washburn NC pp. 104–105).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the late Major Counselor's residence; co-present.

c.name: The Secret Love's residence
r.name: The Secret Love's residence
e.evidence: Leaving the nun's residence at dawn, Genji pauses at the gate of a woman he had been visiting in secret and has an escort "recite a poem that played upon the saibara, 'My Lover's Gate'" (Washburn NC pp. 106–107).
d.evidence: Recited aloud at her gate; "a comely servant emerged and answered for her lady" (Washburn NC p. 107).

c.name: The Secret Love's residence
r.name: The Secret Love's residence
e.evidence: "A comely servant emerged and answered for her lady" from within the residence (Washburn NC p. 107).
d.evidence: Conveyed at the gate to Genji, outside; he then returns to Nijō (Washburn NC p. 107).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, making a practice album for the girl, Genji adds his own verse in small characters beside a Kokin rokujō poem on purple paper (Washburn NC pp. 111–112).
d.evidence: Shown to Murasaki, present beside him at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Encouraged to practice, the girl writes the verse at Nijō, then tries to hide it before Genji "made her show it to him" (Washburn NC p. 112).
d.evidence: Read by Genji, present at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Having trailed Genji from the palace, Tō no Chūjō waits by the broken fence of the Hitachi villa; when Genji tries to slip away, he approaches and recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 116–117).
d.evidence: Spoken to Genji at the fence of the Hitachi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Genji, amused on recognizing Tō no Chūjō, answers on the spot at the fence (Washburn NC p. 117).
d.evidence: Heard by Tō no Chūjō, present at the Hitachi villa; co-present. The two then ride off together to Sanjō.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Admitted to the space under the eaves, with the Princess behind a screen and unresponsive, Genji recites the verse (Washburn NC p. 121).
d.evidence: Spoken to the Princess, present behind the screen at the Hitachi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Jijū, the nurse's daughter, unable to bear her lady's silence, "moved over closer . . . and replied for her" (Washburn NC p. 121).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Hitachi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Genji replies in person, then continues his overtures (Washburn NC pp. 121–122).
d.evidence: Heard by the Princess, present at the Hitachi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: After a day "in service at the palace," Genji "finally managed to get a letter off to her that evening" (Washburn NC pp. 122–123). Composition location inferred from context — the writing spot is not stated; Genji is in his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo).
d.evidence: Delivered to the Princess at the Hitachi villa, where "the expected morning-after letter arrived in the evening" (Washburn NC p. 123).

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: At her attendants' prompting, the Princess "wrote out the poem on purple paper" at the Hitachi villa (Washburn NC p. 123).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji, who "put the letter aside" (Washburn NC p. 123). Receipt location inferred from context — his location that evening is not pinned (the palace, the Kiritsubo).

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: On the snowy morning, having seen the Princess's looks at last, Genji makes ready to leave and recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 126–127).
d.evidence: Spoken to the Princess, present at the Hitachi villa; co-present. She "simply giggled inanely" and did not reply.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Held up at the warped middle gate, watching the old gatekeeper struggle in the snow, Genji "composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 128).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly), at the gate of the Hitachi villa.

c.name: The Hitachi villa
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: The Princess's New Year gift-poem, written at the Hitachi villa on rustic Michinoku paper and (this time) composed by herself (Washburn NC pp. 129–130).
d.evidence: Brought by Taifu, who "called on Genji at his quarters in the palace," with the accompanying clothing box (Washburn NC pp. 128–129).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: At his palace quarters, Genji "spread open her letter and began scribbling on the edge of the paper . . . as if he were casually practicing his writing" (Washburn NC pp. 129–130).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (jotted privately at his palace quarters, the Kiritsubo); Taifu sneaks a look (Washburn NC pp. 129–130).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: Taifu, present with Genji at his palace quarters, "responded" to his jotted verse (Washburn NC p. 130).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Kiritsubo; co-present.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Hitachi villa
e.evidence: Genji's reply, "a real strain . . . to come up with something"; the next day he hands it to Taifu at the Seiryōden attendants' waiting room to deliver (Washburn NC pp. 130–131). Composition location inferred from context — the writing spot is not stated; Genji's palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo).
d.evidence: Delivered to the Hitachi villa, where the Princess's ladies "gathered round and looked at it admiringly" (Washburn NC p. 131).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Back at Nijō — "Arriving at his residence in Nijō, he found Murasaki" — playing with her, Genji eyes the early red plum "next to the covered stairs" and sighs the verse (Washburn NC p. 133).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly), at Nijō.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
e.evidence: The morning after the Seigaiha dress rehearsal, "The following morning Genji sent a note to Fujitsubo" (Washburn NC p. 135). Composition location inferred from context — Genji's writing spot is not stated; his palace-quarters period (the Kiritsubo).
d.evidence: Sent to Fujitsubo, still resident at court — she withdraws to her Sanjō villa only "around the time of the procession" (Washburn NC p. 136). Receipt location inferred from context — her court pavilion (the Higyōsha); the exact room is not stated.

c.name: Fujitsubo's palace residence (the Higyōsha)
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: Moved by the dance, Fujitsubo "could not very well pretend she had not seen his note, and so she replied" (Washburn NC p. 135). Composition location inferred from context — her court pavilion; the exact room is not stated.
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji, who "had not expected a reply, and so he was ecstatic" (Washburn NC p. 135). Receipt location inferred from context — his palace quarters (the Kiritsubo); not stated.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: Visiting the recuperating Fujitsubo — "She had withdrawn to her own villa on Sanjō Avenue, and so he called on her there" (Washburn NC p. 137) — Genji pleads through Ōmyōbu and recites the verse on the verge of tears (Washburn NC pp. 141–142).
d.evidence: Heard by Ōmyōbu, present at Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: Unable to refuse him outright, Ōmyōbu answers in person, recalling Kanesuke's poem on parents "lost in darkness" (Washburn NC p. 142).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: "Genji returned to his Nijō villa," had a pink picked from the front garden, and "sent [it] to Ōmyōbu" with the verse for Fujitsubo (Washburn NC p. 143).
d.evidence: Ōmyōbu "showed it to Fujitsubo and encouraged her lady to write back" at her Sanjō villa (Washburn NC p. 143).

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Deeply moved, Fujitsubo "sent back a simple poem written in the faintest of hands" from her Sanjō villa (Washburn NC p. 143).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji. Receipt location inferred from context — he had written from his Nijō villa (Washburn NC p. 143); his receiving spot is not stated.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: In the Emperor's residential quarters, after Naishi "had finished combing and arranging the Emperor's hair" and His Majesty "withdrew to change," "Genji now found himself alone with Naishi" (Washburn NC p. 146); they exchange fans and she recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 146–147).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Seiryōden; co-present.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: At the Seiryōden, where the Emperor has withdrawn to change his robes and Genji is left alone with Gen no Naishi (p. 146), Genji replies to her in person (Washburn NC p. 147); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Naishi, present at the Seiryōden; co-present.

c.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
r.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
e.evidence: On a rainy evening Genji "moved closer to her chambers" and hears Naishi at her biwa; she takes up a saibara and sings the verse (Washburn NC pp. 148–149).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji from just outside her chambers; effectively co-present.

c.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
r.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
e.evidence: In Gen no Naishi’s chambers at the palace, which Genji enters at her invitation, he answers her in person (Washburn NC p. 149); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Naishi, present; co-present.

c.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
r.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
e.evidence: After "Tō no Chūjō stealthily slipped inside her chambers" and the cloak was ripped, he taunts Genji with the verse (Washburn NC p. 150).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present; co-present.

c.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
r.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
e.evidence: In Gen no Naishi’s chambers, as Genji and Tō no Chūjō struggle over their robes, Genji retorts in person (Washburn NC p. 150); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Tō no Chūjō, present at Gen no Naishi's chambers; co-present.

c.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: The following morning Naishi "had an obi sash and a pair of trousers returned to Genji, along with a note" bearing the verse, from her chambers (Washburn NC pp. 150–151).
d.evidence: Carried to Genji the morning after. Receipt location inferred from context — his whereabouts are not stated; his palace quarters (the Kiritsubo).

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Gen no Naishi's chambers at the palace
e.evidence: Irritated but sympathetic, "He replied" (Washburn NC p. 151). Composition location inferred from context — Genji's writing spot is not stated; his palace quarters (the Kiritsubo).
d.evidence: Returned to Naishi. Receipt location inferred from context — her palace chambers; not stated at delivery.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: Tō no Chūjō's billet at the palace
e.evidence: Genji wraps Tō no Chūjō's light-blue obi "in paper of matching color and sent it on" with the verse (Washburn NC p. 151). Composition location inferred from context — Genji's writing spot is not stated; his palace quarters (the Kiritsubo).
d.evidence: "Tō no Chūjō was at his billet, where he had been waiting to go on duty for the Palace Guard" (Washburn NC p. 151).

c.name: Tō no Chūjō's billet at the palace
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: "Tō no Chūjō immediately shot back" his reply from his billet (Washburn NC p. 151).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji. Receipt location inferred from context — his palace quarters (the Kiritsubo); not stated.

c.name: The Palace
r.name: The Palace
e.evidence: On the night Fujitsubo formally enters the palace as Empress, Genji — in her retinue — watches her palanquin borne away and "muttered a poem to himself" (Washburn NC pp. 152–153).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Shishinden
r.name: The Shishinden
e.evidence: At the cherry-blossom banquet in "the courtyard on the south side of the Shishinden" (Washburn NC p. 154), Fujitsubo gazes out at Genji; "Since Fujitsubo composed this privately, I wonder how it came to be known to the court?" (Washburn NC p. 154).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Kokiden
r.name: The Kokiden
e.evidence: After the banquet Genji "moved down the narrow passageway toward the Kokiden . . . the Kokiden was deserted"; finding a young woman murmuring a misty-moon line, he murmurs his verse (Washburn NC pp. 154–155).
d.evidence: Spoken to her, present at the Kokiden; he carries her into the chamber. Co-present.

c.name: The Kokiden
r.name: The Kokiden
e.evidence: At the Kokiden, in the passageway where Genji has carried Oborozukiyo (pp. 154–155), she answers him in person when pressed for her name (Washburn NC p. 155); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Kokiden; co-present.

c.name: The Kokiden
r.name: The Kokiden
e.evidence: At the Kokiden, Genji answers Oborozukiyo in person, the two together (Washburn NC p. 156); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by her, present at the Kokiden; co-present. They exchange fans, then part as attendants stir.

c.name: The Kiritsubo
r.name: The Kiritsubo
e.evidence: After the second-day banquet at the palace, brooding over the fan Oborozukiyo gave him, Genji "wrote a poem on her fan and set it aside" (Washburn NC p. 157). Location inferred from context — the palace is the established banquet setting; his specific quarters (the Kiritsubo) and the writing spot are not stated.
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee; written on the fan and kept, not sent.

c.name: The Minister of the Right's residence
r.name: The palace
e.evidence: Disappointed by Genji's absence from his wisteria banquet, the Minister of the Right "dispatched one of his sons, a Lesser Captain of the fourth rank, with a message" bearing the verse (Washburn NC p. 158).
d.evidence: "Genji was at the palace when the message arrived, and so he showed it to his father" (Washburn NC p. 159).

c.name: The Minister of the Right's residence
r.name: The Minister of the Right's residence
e.evidence: At the banquet, Genji edges to the southeastern corner of the main hall, takes the silent lady's hand through the curtains, and recites the verse, having guessed who she is (Washburn NC pp. 159–160).
d.evidence: Spoken to her through the curtains, present; co-present.

c.name: The Minister of the Right's residence
r.name: The Minister of the Right's residence
e.evidence: At the Minister of the Right’s residence, where Genji sits at the southeastern corner doors of the main hall (pp. 159–160), Oborozukiyo answers him in person, her voice confirming her identity (Washburn NC p. 160); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Minister of the Right's residence; co-present.

c.name: The lustration procession (the carriage quarrel)
r.name: The lustration procession (the carriage quarrel)
e.evidence: Shoved to the rear in the carriage quarrel and "coldly" passed by Genji "without so much as a glance," Lady Rokujō — "in an agony of anger and indignation" — composes the verse on the spot (Washburn NC pp. 163–164).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: On the festival day at Nijō, Genji trims Murasaki's hair and recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 166–167).
d.evidence: Spoken to Murasaki, present at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, Genji’s own villa, Murasaki “chose to write out her reply” with him present (Washburn NC p. 167); co-present.
d.evidence: Read by Genji, present at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
r.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
e.evidence: From the carriage drawn up beside Genji's at the festival viewing ground, Naishi writes the verse "on a piece of her folding fan" (Washburn NC p. 167).
d.evidence: Passed to Genji in the adjacent carriage at the Kamo Festival viewing ground; co-present.

c.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
r.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
e.evidence: Recognizing Naishi's hand, Genji "sent back a curt reply" (Washburn NC p. 167).
d.evidence: Received by Naishi in the adjacent carriage at the Kamo Festival viewing ground; co-present.

c.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
r.name: The Kamo Festival (the viewing ground)
e.evidence: At the Kamo Festival viewing ground, the carriages drawn up side by side, Naishi answers Genji again across the gap (Washburn NC pp. 167–168); co-present.
d.evidence: Received by Genji at the Kamo Festival viewing ground; co-present.

c.name: The site of Lady Rokujō's healing rites
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Secluded where her healing rites are held — she "had to move out of her residence and have the rites performed elsewhere," "not at her usual residence in Rokujō" (Washburn NC p. 169) — Lady Rokujō answers Genji's curt note "with no poem attached" (Washburn NC pp. 169–170).
d.evidence: Carried to Genji at the Sanjō residence, where he is attending his ailing wife: "I really must stay here" (Washburn NC p. 170).

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The site of Lady Rokujō's healing rites
e.evidence: Moved by her script, Genji "sent his reply well after dark" from the Sanjō residence, where he is attending his wife (Washburn NC p. 170).
d.evidence: Carried back to Lady Rokujō at the site of her healing rites.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Forced to show itself, the obsessive spirit speaks through Genji's wife and recites the verse; Genji then beholds the face of Lady Rokujō (Washburn NC pp. 172–173).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at his wife's bedside in the Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: When dawn arrived after the Toribeno cremation, "they had to return home . . . as Genji gazed up into that dawn sky he was moved to compose the following" (Washburn NC p. 176). Location inferred from context — composed on the dawn return; the exact spot is not stated, and only at the next verse has he "arriv[ed] back at his quarters . . . in Sanjō" (Washburn NC p. 176).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly); same location, inferred.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: "After arriving back at his quarters at the Minister's residence in Sanjō," unable to sleep and donning gray mourning robes, Genji composes the verse (Washburn NC p. 176).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At dawn an attendant brings Genji a mourning-gray letter tied to chrysanthemums in Lady Rokujō's hand (Washburn NC pp. 177–178). Composition location inferred from context — her whereabouts in this period are not stated; her Rokujō residence.
d.evidence: Received by Genji at the Sanjō residence during his mourning.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
e.evidence: After long hesitation, Genji replies on purple paper tinged with gray, from the Sanjō residence (Washburn NC p. 178).
d.evidence: Carried back to Lady Rokujō. Receipt location inferred from context — her Rokujō residence; her whereabouts are not stated.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: On a rainy evening Tō no Chūjō calls on Genji in his mourning seclusion; gazing at the garden, he recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 179–180).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Minister of the Left's Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At the Minister of the Left’s residence on Sanjō, Genji answers Tō no Chūjō in person (Washburn NC p. 180); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Tō no Chūjō, present at the Minister of the Left's Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At the same Sanjō residence, Genji has the child’s nurse Saishō carry gentians and pinks with the verse attached to his mother-in-law in her chambers (Washburn NC pp. 180–181).
d.evidence: Brought to Princess Ōmiya in her chambers within the same residence.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Weeping, Princess Ōmiya answers (Washburn NC p. 181).
d.evidence: Returned to Genji within the residence.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: Still that evening, Genji sends a note to Princess Asagao on sky-tinted Chinese paper from the Sanjō residence (Washburn NC p. 181).
d.evidence: Her ladies-in-waiting read it to her at her residence: "This is one letter you cannot ignore" (Washburn NC p. 181).

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Asagao replies in faint, deliberately light brushstrokes from her residence (Washburn NC p. 181).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji at the Sanjō residence.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Among the calligraphy practice Genji left behind in his Sanjō quarters, under a Song of Everlasting Sorrow line, the Minister finds this verse (Washburn NC pp. 184–185).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (practice writing, left behind at Sanjō).

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: A second verse among the same papers at Sanjō, under another Song of Everlasting Sorrow line (Washburn NC p. 185).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (practice writing left among his papers at the Sanjō residence) (Washburn NC p. 185).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: The morning after their first night, Genji leaves "his betrothal note folded in a love knot at her pillow" at Nijō (Washburn NC p. 187).
d.evidence: Murasaki "finally lifted her head and found his betrothal note . . . at her pillow," opening it in her quarters at Nijō (Washburn NC p. 187).

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: On a New Year's visit to Sanjō, Genji dons the robes Princess Ōmiya prepared and replies to her note with the verse (Washburn NC pp. 190–191).
d.evidence: Returned to Princess Ōmiya within the residence.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Princess Ōmiya answers — an old woman's ceaseless tears (Washburn NC p. 191).
d.evidence: Received by Genji at the Sanjō residence.

c.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
r.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
e.evidence: Genji presents a sakaki branch at her blind at the Nonomiya shrine; she answers, questioning his coming (Washburn NC pp. 193–194).
d.evidence: Spoken to Genji at her blind in the Nonomiya shrine; co-present.

c.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
r.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
e.evidence: At the temporary shrine at Nonomiya, where Genji sits at Lady Rokujō’s blind (pp. 192–194), he replies to her in person, the sacred branch in hand (Washburn NC p. 194); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Rokujō, present at the Nonomiya shrine; co-present.

c.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
r.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
e.evidence: At Nonomiya, at dawn, Genji takes Lady Rokujō’s hand and recites to her in person (Washburn NC pp. 194–195); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Rokujō, present at the Nonomiya shrine; co-present.

c.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
r.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
e.evidence: Rokujō answers the dawn parting (Washburn NC p. 195).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Nonomiya shrine; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
e.evidence: As Akikonomu's party departs, "a letter arrived from Genji . . . addressed 'To the High Priestess, with Humble Reverence' and bound . . . with a ritual cord made of paper mulberry bark" (Washburn NC p. 196). Composition location inferred from context — Genji "kept to himself" at his Nijō villa (Washburn NC p. 197); his exact spot is not stated.
d.evidence: Read at the temporary shrine as Akikonomu's party sets out (Washburn NC p. 196).

c.name: The temporary shrine at Nonomiya (the plains of Sagano)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Though "extremely busy with her preparations, the High Priestess replied. Her Head of Household wrote out the response" at the temporary shrine (Washburn NC p. 196).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji. Receipt location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his exact spot is not stated.

c.name: The palace (the Ceremony of Parting)
r.name: The palace (the Ceremony of Parting)
e.evidence: Borne to the palace for the Ceremony of Parting, Rokujō recalls coming here once as a bride and composes to herself (Washburn NC p. 196).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Ise departure procession (passing Genji's Nijō villa)
e.evidence: As the Ise procession passes Genji's villa, he attaches the poem to a sakaki branch and sends it out (Washburn NC p. 196).
d.evidence: Carried to Rokujō in the departing procession as it passes Nijō.

c.name: The barrier gate at Ōsaka (Ōsaka no seki)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: "Her reply, which she sent from the barrier gate at Ōsaka, did not reach him until the following day" (Washburn NC p. 196).
d.evidence: Reaches Genji at Nijō the following day (Washburn NC p. 196).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Staring out at the misty dawn after the procession has gone, Genji "whispered a poem to himself" at Nijō (Washburn NC pp. 196–197).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
r.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
e.evidence: At the late Emperor's villa, as Fujitsubo prepares to move out, Prince Hyōbu gazes at a snow-laden five-needle pine and "composed a poem" (Washburn NC p. 199).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the late Retired Emperor's villa; co-present.

c.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
r.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
e.evidence: Genji, weeping, replies, gazing at the frozen pond (Washburn NC p. 199).
d.evidence: Heard by Prince Hyōbu, present at the late Retired Emperor's villa; co-present.

c.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
r.name: The late Retired Emperor's villa
e.evidence: At the late Retired Emperor’s villa, before the snow-laden pine, Ōmyōbu adds a verse of her own among those exchanged there (Washburn NC p. 199); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by those present at the late Retired Emperor's villa; co-present.

c.name: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)
r.name: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)
e.evidence: At a dawn tryst in the palace passageway where they first met, a guard's voice marks the hour and Oborozukiyo composes (Washburn NC pp. 201–202).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the palace; co-present.

c.name: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)
r.name: The palace (the passageway near the Kokiden)
e.evidence: In the passageway near the Kokiden, at their dawn tryst, Genji answers Oborozukiyo in person as he must leave (Washburn NC p. 202); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Oborozukiyo, present at the palace; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: Having forced his way to her and hidden through her seizure, Genji at dawn laments his fate at Fujitsubo's family residence on Sanjō (Washburn NC p. 204).
d.evidence: Heard by Fujitsubo, present; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: At Fujitsubo’s family residence on Sanjō, to which she has withdrawn, she answers Genji in person, brushing his words aside (Washburn NC p. 204); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Fujitsubo's Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: On retreat at the Urin'in, Genji sends Murasaki a letter on Michinokuni paper with the verse (Washburn NC p. 207).
d.evidence: Murasaki weeps as she reads it at Nijō (Washburn NC p. 207).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)
e.evidence: Murasaki "sent her reply, which consisted of a single poem written on white paper" from Nijō (Washburn NC p. 207).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji at the Urin'in.

c.name: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)
r.name: The Kamo Shrine
e.evidence: From the nearby Urin'in, Genji sends a sakaki-branch letter to the Kamo Priestess, Asagao, via her attendant Chūjō (Washburn NC pp. 207–208).
d.evidence: Conveyed to Asagao at the Kamo Shrine.

c.name: The Kamo Shrine
r.name: The Urin'in Temple (north of the capital)
e.evidence: Asagao replies on a strip of mulberry-bark cloth from the Kamo Shrine (Washburn NC p. 208).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji at the Urin'in.

c.name: The palace (Fujitsubo's withdrawal)
r.name: The palace (Fujitsubo's withdrawal)
e.evidence: The night Fujitsubo withdraws from the palace after visiting the Crown Prince, she has Ōmyōbu take a poem to Genji, who is close by (Washburn NC p. 211).
d.evidence: Read by Genji, present at the palace; co-present.

c.name: The palace (Fujitsubo's withdrawal)
r.name: The palace (Fujitsubo's withdrawal)
e.evidence: At the palace, on the night of Fujitsubo’s withdrawal, Genji — in attendance close by — replies through Ōmyōbu (Washburn NC p. 211); co-present.
d.evidence: Conveyed to Fujitsubo at the palace, close by; co-present.

c.name: The palace (Oborozukiyo's quarters)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: As the early winter rains near, Genji "received a poem from her" (Washburn NC pp. 211–212). Composition location inferred from context — Oborozukiyo writes "secretly in this melancholy season" from her palace quarters (the Kokiden, Washburn NC p. 210); her exact spot is not stated.
d.evidence: Received by Genji at his villa — "the cabinet where he stored his writing implements," "the women who were in service to him" (Washburn NC p. 212); he has her messenger wait and selects fine paper.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The palace (Oborozukiyo's quarters)
e.evidence: Genji writes a careful reply on "Chinese paper from the cabinet where he stored his writing implements," among "the women who were in service to him" at his Nijō villa (Washburn NC p. 212).
d.evidence: Carried back to Oborozukiyo. Receipt location inferred from context — her palace quarters; her exact spot is not stated.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: On the first anniversary of the late Emperor's death, "on the day of the anniversary, heavy snow fell. Genji sent this poem to Fujitsubo" (Washburn NC p. 212). Composition location inferred from context — his Nijō villa, where he kept his devotions; his spot is not stated.
d.evidence: Received by Fujitsubo at her family residence on Sanjō (Washburn NC p. 212).

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Fujitsubo sends a reply from her Sanjō residence (Washburn NC p. 212).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji. Receipt location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his spot is not stated.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: After Fujitsubo stuns the assembly by taking vows at the close of the Eight Lectures, Genji, unable to speak freely, conveys this verse at her Sanjō residence (Washburn NC pp. 213–214).
d.evidence: Conveyed to Fujitsubo, where the rite was held; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: At Fujitsubo’s Sanjō residence, with Genji present, she replies, owning her lingering attachments — her words softened by her messenger (Washburn NC p. 214); co-present.
d.evidence: Conveyed to Genji, present at Fujitsubo's Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: Visiting Fujitsubo's spare new nun's quarters after the New Year, Genji murmurs the verse (Washburn NC pp. 215–216).
d.evidence: Heard by Fujitsubo, near the curtain; co-present.

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: At her Sanjō residence, Fujitsubo answers Genji in a subdued voice, the two together (Washburn NC p. 216); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Fujitsubo's Sanjō residence; co-present.

c.name: Tō no Chūjō's residence
r.name: Tō no Chūjō's residence
e.evidence: At the banquet two days after the rhyme contest, as Tō no Chūjō's son finishes "Takasago," "Tō no Chūjō offered a cup to Genji with this poem" (Washburn NC pp. 218–219). Venue inferred from context — the banquet "Tō no Chūjō sponsored" two days after the contest at Genji's villa is not named (Washburn NC p. 218).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present; co-present.

c.name: Tō no Chūjō's residence
r.name: Tō no Chūjō's residence
e.evidence: Smiling, Genji takes the cup and answers, teasing that he is "in decline" (Washburn NC p. 219). Venue inferred from context — see 10SK32; the banquet is not named.
d.evidence: Heard by Tō no Chūjō, present; co-present.

c.name: A woman's house at Nakagawa
r.name: A woman's house at Nakagawa
e.evidence: Hearing a koto from a house he had once visited near the Nakagawa, Genji "had his carriage backed up and . . . gave Koremitsu a poem and sent him in to make inquiries" (Washburn NC p. 223).
d.evidence: Koremitsu "presented his lord's message" to the woman within; the younger women find it suspicious (Washburn NC p. 223).

c.name: A woman's house at Nakagawa
r.name: A woman's house at Nakagawa
e.evidence: The woman, once the object of Genji's affections, "replied" with feigned uncertainty from within the house (Washburn NC p. 223).
d.evidence: Relayed by Koremitsu back to Genji at the gate; he takes his leave (Washburn NC p. 223).

c.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
r.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
e.evidence: Late at night at the villa, as a cuckoo calls and orange blossoms scent the air, Genji recites the verse to the former Reikeiden Consort (Washburn NC p. 224).
d.evidence: Heard by the Consort, present; co-present.

c.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
r.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
e.evidence: At the Reikeiden Consort’s villa, the Consort answers Genji in person with a single, deeply felt verse (Washburn NC p. 224); co-present.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Reikeiden Consort's villa; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: Genji "had called at the Minister of the Left's residence" and there, at dawn, "whispered a poem that was not meant as a reply" (Washburn NC pp. 227–228).
d.evidence: Saishō "brought tidings from the Minister's wife, Princess Ōmiya," and "Genji sent his reply to Princess Ōmiya" in the same residence (Washburn NC p. 228).

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: "Princess Ōmiya sent a poem in reply to his note," from the same residence (Washburn NC p. 228).
d.evidence: Conveyed to Genji, who had "decided to stay over in his late wife's quarters" there (Washburn NC p. 227).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At the mirror stand Genji "moved over to the mirror stand to arrange his hair" and spoke the verse before Murasaki (Washburn NC p. 230). Location inferred from context — the scene is at Genji's villa, named "his Nijō villa" (NC p. 231); prefilled comp/receipt (Nijō Palace) agrees.
d.evidence: Murasaki present: "Noticing the tears in Murasaki's eyes" (Washburn NC p. 230). Location inferred from context (Nijō; prefilled receipt agrees).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: "Murasaki replied," in person (Washburn NC p. 230). Location inferred from context (Nijō, NC p. 231).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present: "She tried to hide her tears from him by sitting behind a pillar" (Washburn NC p. 230). Location inferred from context (Nijō).

c.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
r.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
e.evidence: In "her quarters on the west side of the villa," Hanachirusato "moved outside her curtains and remained seated, looking up at the sky" (Washburn NC pp. 230–231).
d.evidence: Genji present at the villa: "They talked until the dawn approached" (Washburn NC p. 231).

c.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
r.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
e.evidence: Genji "comforted her with his reply," in person at the villa (Washburn NC p. 231).
d.evidence: Heard by Hanachirusato, present; "He departed as dawn broke" (Washburn NC p. 231).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: "Genji took the extraordinary step of communicating with Oborozukiyo" by letter (Washburn NC pp. 231–232). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at his Nijō villa amid departure preparations, NC p. 231).
d.evidence: A letter, lest "the letter might be discovered by the Kokiden faction"; her whereabouts are not stated (Washburn NC p. 232).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Her reply "produced at a moment of great distress"; her location is not stated (Washburn NC p. 232).
d.evidence: Returned to Genji as part of the secret correspondence (Washburn NC p. 232). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Nijō, NC p. 231).

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: Genji went "to call on Fujitsubo to take leave of her... seated right in front of her blinds, and she spoke to him directly" (Washburn NC p. 232).
d.evidence: Genji present at her blinds: "she spoke to him directly, with no intermediary" (Washburn NC p. 232).

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: "Genji responded with a poem," in person at her blinds (Washburn NC p. 233).
d.evidence: Heard by Fujitsubo, present, in the same leave-taking (Washburn NC pp. 232–233).

c.name: The road by the Lower Kamo Shrine
r.name: The road by the Lower Kamo Shrine
e.evidence: "Catching sight of the Lower Kamo Shrine in the distance," the Lesser Captain "dismounted... and composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 233).
d.evidence: Composed to himself on the road (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 233).

c.name: The Kamo Shrine
r.name: The Kamo Shrine
e.evidence: Genji "dismounted as well and, facing the Kamo Shrine, prayed for the blessings of the gods" (Washburn NC p. 233).
d.evidence: Addressed to the gods of Tadasu Woods, before whom he stood (Washburn NC pp. 233–234).

c.name: The Retired Emperor's tomb (hills north of the capital)
r.name: The Retired Emperor's tomb (hills north of the capital)
e.evidence: "When he arrived at the tomb in the hills," a vision of his father appeared and Genji composed (Washburn NC p. 234).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at the tomb (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 234).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Crown Prince's quarters at the palace
e.evidence: "Returning to his Nijō villa just after sunrise, he sent a note to the Crown Prince" (Washburn NC p. 234).
d.evidence: Genji "gave instructions that the letter be delivered to her [Ōmyōbu's] quarters"; "The Crown Prince... read what Genji had written" (Washburn NC p. 234).

c.name: The Crown Prince's quarters at the palace
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Ōmyōbu "added her own reply to the Crown Prince's note" from his quarters at the palace (Washburn NC p. 235).
d.evidence: Returned to Genji as part of "the exchange of letters" (Washburn NC p. 235). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Nijō, NC p. 234).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: On the departure night, Genji "rolled up the blind and coaxed her out onto the veranda" and recited (Washburn NC p. 236). Location inferred from context (Nijō, NC p. 231).
d.evidence: Murasaki present: "Bathed in the moonlight, she looked beautiful beyond words" (Washburn NC p. 236). Location inferred from context (Nijō).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: "Murasaki, however, responded as follows," in person (Washburn NC p. 236). Location inferred from context (Nijō; prefilled comp/receipt (Nijō Palace) agrees).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present: "Knowing how she truly felt, Genji found it nearly impossible to leave her" (Washburn NC p. 236). Location inferred from context (Nijō).

c.name: On the journey to Suma (the ruined Ōe villa, Yodo River)
r.name: On the journey to Suma (the ruined Ōe villa, Yodo River)
e.evidence: On the journey, "He viewed the site of the Ōe villa on the banks of the Yodo River" and composed (Washburn NC p. 236).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly during the journey (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 236).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Having "managed to arrive at Suma," gazing back, "he saw the mountains, obscure in the mist," and composed (Washburn NC pp. 236–237).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at Suma (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 237).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
e.evidence: He "dispatched messages to the capital... He wrote the following to Fujitsubo" (Washburn NC p. 238). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Carried to Fujitsubo, who sends a reply (12SM22) (Washburn NC pp. 238–239). Receipt location inferred from context (her residence, where Genji took leave of her at her blinds, NC p. 232).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: Genji "enclosed a private missive to her inside a letter addressed to her lady-in-waiting, Chūnagon" (Washburn NC p. 238). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Sent via Chūnagon, "the attendant who had acted earlier as their go-between"; Oborozukiyo's location is not stated (Washburn NC p. 238).

c.name: Fujitsubo's villa on Sanjō Avenue
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Fujitsubo's "reply to him was suffused with somewhat more emotion than usual" (Washburn NC p. 239). Composition location inferred from context (her residence, NC p. 232).
d.evidence: Delivered to Genji in reply to his letter (Washburn NC pp. 238–239). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "a brief reply and poem from Oborozukiyo," tucked inside Chūnagon's letter; her location is not stated (Washburn NC p. 239).
d.evidence: Genji read it: "Some of the details touched Genji so deeply that he broke down and wept" (Washburn NC p. 239). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "Murasaki sent a long reply to Genji's heartfelt letter" (Washburn NC pp. 238–239). Composition location inferred from context (Nijō, where she remained, NC p. 231).
d.evidence: Delivered to Genji among his women's replies (Washburn NC p. 239). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).

c.name: The Ise Shrine
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "Genji had sent a messenger to the Ise Shrine... and she had in turn... [dispatched] her own messenger" (Washburn NC p. 239).
d.evidence: Genji read it: "her letter arrived at a time when he was thinking nostalgically" (Washburn NC pp. 240–241). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).

c.name: The Ise Shrine
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Rokujō "had attached a second poem" to the same letter from Ise (Washburn NC p. 240).
d.evidence: Reached Genji with her first poem (Washburn NC pp. 240–241). Receipt location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: The Ise Shrine
e.evidence: "One can just imagine the words Genji used in composing his reply" (Washburn NC p. 241). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Sent to Rokujō, who is "remaining here among the deities at this Shinto shrine" at Ise (Washburn NC pp. 240–241).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: The Ise Shrine
e.evidence: Genji's second verse in the same reply (Washburn NC p. 241). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Sent to Rokujō at the Ise Shrine (Washburn NC p. 241).

c.name: The Reikeiden Consort's villa
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Hanachirusato, "at the villa of the scattering orange blossoms," "conveyed her feelings to him" (Washburn NC p. 241).
d.evidence: Read by Genji "in the rustic setting of Suma" (Washburn NC p. 241).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Lying awake, Genji played his koto, then "abruptly stopped and murmured the following poem" (Washburn NC p. 243). Location inferred from context (Genji's villa at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Soliloquy overheard: "Hearing his poem, his attendants were startled awake" (Washburn NC p. 243). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: As geese crossed the evening sky, "Genji composed a verse" aloud (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Genji's villa at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Heard by his companions, who respond in turn (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "Yoshikiyo responded," in person (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).
d.evidence: Heard by those present (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "Koremitsu also responded," in person (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).
d.evidence: Heard by those present (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: The Lesser Captain "composed yet another poem," in person (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).
d.evidence: Heard by those present (Washburn NC p. 244). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: On the fifteenth night, "he could not bring himself to retire," and composed (Washburn NC pp. 244–245). Location inferred from context (Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 245). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Recalling Suzaku and Michizane's line, Genji composed again (Washburn NC p. 245). Location inferred from context (Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 245). Location inferred from context (Suma).

c.name: The Gosechi dancer's barge (passing Suma)

e.evidence: As "the boat was being towed past Suma," the former Gosechi dancer "contrived to get a note off to Genji" (Washburn NC pp. 245–246).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: The Gosechi dancer's barge (passing Suma)
e.evidence: "Genji read this with a wry smile" and replied (Washburn NC p. 246). Composition location inferred from context (Genji at Suma, NC p. 237).
d.evidence: Sent back to the barge "towed past Suma": "Having received such an elegant reply, the Gosechi dancer was thrilled" (Washburn NC pp. 245–246).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Seeing smoke "quite near his villa," "It was all such a marvel to him that he composed a poem" (Washburn NC p. 247).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at his villa (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 247).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "Bright moonlight was streaming in, illuminating every corner of Genji's humble, temporary lodgings"; he composed (Washburn NC p. 248).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee, composed inwardly at his Suma lodgings — “He intoned this verse to himself” (Washburn NC p. 248).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: At dawn, plovers crying, "he lay there murmuring the following poem over and over to himself" at his lodging (Washburn NC p. 248).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at his lodging (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 248).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: "The young cherry trees Genji had planted after arriving in Suma were putting forth blossoms"; recalling the Shishinden, he composed (Washburn NC pp. 249–250).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at Suma (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 250).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)

r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
