c.name: The shore at Suma (the purification site)
r.name: The shore at Suma (the purification site)
e.evidence: "Genji, who had wanted to go view the shore in any case, headed down to the sea"; watching the purification doll, "Watching it float away," he composed (Washburn NC p. 252).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at the shore (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 252).

c.name: The shore at Suma (the purification site)
r.name: The shore at Suma (the purification site)
e.evidence: "Sitting in the midst of the bright, cheerful scenery... he composed another poem" at the shore (Washburn NC p. 252).
d.evidence: Composed inwardly at the shore (speaker = addressee) (Washburn NC p. 252).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: Murasaki's letter is carried by "a messenger from his Nijō villa" — "The man carried a letter from Murasaki" (Washburn NC p. 254); composed at Nijō, whence the messenger came.
d.evidence: Read by Genji in the storm at Suma: "After opening her letter, his mood turned to dark despair" (Washburn NC p. 254); the man reports the conditions "here at Suma" (Washburn NC pp. 254–255).

c.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
r.name: Suma (Genji's villa in exile)
e.evidence: After the storm, "he gazed off toward the shore at the roiling surf left in the wake of the storm"; "Feeling forlorn, he composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 256).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: After "Genji had settled in" at the Akashi villa, "he wrote several letters to people in the capital," his reply to Murasaki among them (Washburn NC pp. 259–260).
d.evidence: Sent to Murasaki in the capital. Receipt location inferred from context — her Nijō villa; the text does not show her receiving this letter.

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: At his seaside residence, "Before his eyes, off in the distance, was the island of Awaji"; he murmurs the verse, then plays a koto that "wafted toward the lady's residence at the foot of the hill" (Washburn NC pp. 261–262).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: The novitiate "hurried over to Genji's residence" (Washburn NC p. 262) and speaks the verse in person, naming "the dawn on Akashi's lonely strand" (Washburn NC p. 264).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Akashi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: Genji answers in person, "In lonely travel robes . . . I wait in sleepless grief for dawn at Akashi" (Washburn NC p. 265).
d.evidence: Heard by the novitiate, present at the Akashi villa; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: "At around noon the following day, Genji sent a letter to the lady's residence at the base of the hill," written on light brown Korean paper (Washburn NC p. 265).
d.evidence: Carried to the Lady's villa, where the novitiate "had already arrived at his daughter's residence and was waiting" when "the letter arrived" (Washburn NC p. 265).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: The Lady refusing to answer, "the novitiate wrote the reply for her" at her residence (Washburn NC pp. 265–266).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji, who was "a little surprised, shocked even, at the seductive allure of the calligraphy" at his seaside villa (Washburn NC p. 266).

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: From his Akashi villa, “The following day Genji sent another note,” on extremely soft, thin paper (Washburn NC p. 266).
d.evidence: Read at the Lady's villa — "when the Akashi lady saw it, she was amazed" (Washburn NC p. 266).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: This time the Lady "did as she was told and replied," writing "on heavily perfumed paper of light purple hue" at her villa (Washburn NC p. 266).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji, who "regarded the lady's letter with delight" at his seaside villa (Washburn NC pp. 266–267).

c.name: The road at Akashi (en route to the Lady's villa)
r.name: The road at Akashi (en route to the Lady's villa)
e.evidence: Riding to the villa, "From the road he could see all around the shore"; brought to mind of Murasaki, "Instinctively, he muttered a poem to himself" (Washburn NC p. 269).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: At the Lady's villa a koto-string sounds; Genji recites, "asking her this and many other things besides, all trying to persuade her to play" (Washburn NC p. 270).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady, present; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: The Lady replies in person at her villa: "She replied" (Washburn NC p. 270).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Akashi Lady's villa; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Genji "was prompted to send Murasaki a letter that was longer and more detailed than usual," adding the verse (Washburn NC p. 271). Composition location inferred from context — his Akashi seaside villa; his spot is not stated at the letter.
d.evidence: Read by Murasaki "back at the Nijō villa" (Washburn NC p. 272); her reply follows (Washburn NC p. 271).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: "Murasaki's reply was innocent and sweet," written from the Nijō villa, where she "began to draw paintings" (Washburn NC pp. 271–272).
d.evidence: Genji "read it over and over, unable to put it down" (Washburn NC p. 271). Receipt location inferred from context — his Akashi seaside villa; his spot is not stated at the receipt.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: Two days before departure, at the Lady's villa, "Genji whispered a poem" (Washburn NC p. 274).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady, present; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: "The Akashi lady replied" in person at her villa (Washburn NC p. 274).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Akashi Lady's villa; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: After Genji asks her to play "one song on the koto . . . as a keepsake," the Lady "replied in an almost inaudible whisper" at her villa (Washburn NC p. 274).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the Akashi Lady's villa; co-present.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: Genji answers in person — "Would that our relationship remain in tune / Like the middle string of this koto I leave" — promising they will meet again (Washburn NC p. 274).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady, present; co-present.

c.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: On the departure morning, having "left the Akashi lady's quarters while it was still dark," Genji "found an opportunity . . . to send her a poem" (Washburn NC p. 275).
d.evidence: Carried to the Lady at her villa; "She replied" (Washburn NC p. 275).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
e.evidence: The Lady "replied" from her villa (Washburn NC p. 275).
d.evidence: Carried to Genji at the departure: "He recognized that she was writing exactly what she felt . . . tears streamed from his eyes" (Washburn NC p. 275).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
e.evidence: A poem from the Lady "had been attached to the hunting robes her father presented to Genji for the journey" (Washburn NC p. 275) — composed at her villa.
d.evidence: Conveyed to Genji at the departure, who "managed to compose this reply" (Washburn NC p. 275).

c.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: At the departure, "Despite all the commotion around him, Genji managed to compose this reply" (Washburn NC p. 275).
d.evidence: Conveyed to the Lady with the robes: Genji "in return gave her the robes he had been wearing" (Washburn NC p. 275).

c.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
r.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
e.evidence: Seeing Genji off at the departure, "The old man composed a poem" (Washburn NC p. 276).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Akashi; co-present.

c.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
r.name: Akashi (Genji's departure for the capital)
e.evidence: Genji answers the novitiate in person at the departure (Washburn NC p. 276).
d.evidence: Heard by the novitiate, present at Akashi; co-present.

c.name: The palace (Genji's audience with Suzaku)
r.name: The palace (Genji's audience with Suzaku)
e.evidence: "A summons from Suzaku arrived, and Genji went to the palace" (Washburn NC p. 278); conversing with Suzaku, "Genji responded with a poem" (Washburn NC p. 278).
d.evidence: Heard by Suzaku, present at the palace; co-present.

c.name: The palace (Genji's audience with Suzaku)
r.name: The palace (Genji's audience with Suzaku)
e.evidence: Suzaku replies in person, "Now that we have gone around the sacred pillars / To meet once again" (Washburn NC p. 278).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the palace; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: Back in the capital, "he sent a letter to the Akashi lady . . . keeping the letter hidden from Murasaki" (Washburn NC p. 279) — written at his Nijō villa.
d.evidence: To be "delivered by the novitiate's men . . . heading back to their province" (Washburn NC p. 279). Receipt location inferred from context — the Akashi Lady's villa; her receipt is not shown.

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: The Gosechi dancer "had a messenger deliver an anonymous letter on the sly" (Washburn NC p. 279). Indeterminate — her whereabouts are not given.
d.evidence: Genji "guessed the identity of the writer . . . He sent this reply" (Washburn NC p. 279). Receipt location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his spot is not stated.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: "He sent this reply" (Washburn NC p. 279). Composition location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his spot is not stated.
d.evidence: Carried to the Gosechi dancer. Indeterminate — her whereabouts are not given.

c.name: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)
r.name: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)
e.evidence: Genji "went in strictest secrecy to visit the woman before she left"; at her ruined residence "within a stand of trees," he recites the verse (Washburn NC pp. 283–284).
d.evidence: Heard by the nurse, present at the nurse's residence; co-present.

c.name: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)
r.name: The nurse's residence (a ruined estate in the capital)
e.evidence: The woman "smiled at his words, then replied" in person at her residence (Washburn NC p. 284).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at the nurse's residence; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: A poem from "his letters to the nurse," in which "he would repeatedly admonish her" about his daughter (Washburn NC p. 284). Composition location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his spot is not stated at the letter.
d.evidence: Sent to the nurse, who had travelled to Akashi (Washburn NC p. 284). Receipt location inferred from context — the Akashi residence; no delivery of this poem is shown.

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: As the messenger prepared to leave, the Lady, "raising her head from where she lay," "wrote down a few of her thoughts and feelings" (Washburn NC p. 285).
d.evidence: Carried back to Genji in the capital. Receipt location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; no reaction at receipt is shown.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: As Genji confesses the Akashi affair, Murasaki "sighed and whispered" the verse, turning away to gaze outside (Washburn NC p. 286).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Genji "responded" in person — "For whose sake did I wander seas and hills" (Washburn NC p. 286).
d.evidence: Heard by Murasaki, present at Nijō; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
e.evidence: For the fiftieth-day celebration "He sent a messenger to Akashi," enclosing the verse (Washburn NC p. 286). Composition location inferred from context — his Nijō villa; his spot is not stated at the letter.
d.evidence: Read at Akashi: "the novitiate wept with joy upon reading the letter"; "The nurse read Genji's letter together with her mistress" (Washburn NC p. 287).

c.name: The Akashi Lady's villa (foot of the hills)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Akashi "The lady's reply included this poem" (Washburn NC p. 287).
d.evidence: Read by Genji at Nijō — he “read her letter over and over, then gave out a long sigh,” Murasaki beside him glancing “sidelong at him” (Washburn NC p. 287).

c.name: Hanachirusato's villa (the scattering orange blossoms)
r.name: Hanachirusato's villa (the scattering orange blossoms)
e.evidence: As Genji visits, "A Water Rail cried out close by, and the lady composed a poem" by the veranda (Washburn NC p. 288).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Hanachirusato's villa; co-present.

c.name: Hanachirusato's villa (the scattering orange blossoms)
r.name: Hanachirusato's villa (the scattering orange blossoms)
e.evidence: Genji answers in person — "If you can be wakened by the Water Rail's cry" (Washburn NC p. 288).
d.evidence: Heard by Hanachirusato, present; co-present.

c.name: Sumiyoshi Shrine
r.name: Sumiyoshi Shrine
e.evidence: As "Genji suddenly emerged from the shrine, Koremitsu approached and presented himself, offering this verse" (Washburn NC p. 292).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present at Sumiyoshi Shrine; co-present.

c.name: Sumiyoshi Shrine
r.name: Sumiyoshi Shrine
e.evidence: Genji answers in person — "Though we were beset by violent wind and waves" (Washburn NC p. 292).
d.evidence: Heard by Koremitsu, present at Sumiyoshi Shrine; co-present.

c.name: Naniwa (the Horie canal)
r.name: Naniwa (the Akashi Lady's boat)
e.evidence: Viewing "the district around the Horie canal," Genji took Koremitsu's brush and "wrote the following on a piece of the folding paper" (Washburn NC p. 292).
d.evidence: An underling "deliver[ed] it to the lady"; though "his letter was brief, she was so touched... she wept" (Washburn NC pp. 292–293).

c.name: Naniwa (Tamino Island)
r.name: Naniwa (the inlet)
e.evidence: The Lady "attached her poem to one of the cords of paper mulberry bark used during the Purification Ritual she underwent on Tamino Island and sent it to Genji" (Washburn NC p. 293).
d.evidence: Reaching Genji at the inlet — “It arrived as the sun was setting,” cranes “calling out from the inlet” (Washburn NC p. 293).

c.name: Naniwa (the inlet)
r.name: Naniwa (the inlet)
e.evidence: At the inlet at sunset — the rising tide and crying cranes about him — Genji frames the verse on "Tamino" and his "travel robes" (Washburn NC p. 293).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
e.evidence: In seclusion at Nijō (he "lowered his blinds and retreated inside"), "as snow and sleet were blowing," Genji "sent a messenger with a note" and verse (Washburn NC p. 296).
d.evidence: Read by Akikonomu at the Rokujō villa, where she “found it startlingly beautiful” and chose paper for her reply (Washburn NC p. 296).

c.name: Lady Rokujō's residence (Rokujō)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Pressed by her women to answer herself, Akikonomu "chose a light gray paper" and wrote the reply at the Rokujō villa (Washburn NC p. 296).
d.evidence: Read by Genji at Nijō, who judged her calligraphy to have “a diffident grace, calm and gentle... an endearing dignity” (Washburn NC p. 296).

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: at the Hitachi villa, the aunt's carriage drawn up "in front of the entrance on the south side of the main hall" (Washburn NC p. 305); there the Princess places the keepsake in a box (p. 306) and gives Jijū this verse before she departs (Washburn NC p. 307).
d.evidence: Heard by Jijū at the Hitachi villa, where she "whispered privately to her lady" before leaving (Washburn NC p. 306); co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: at the Hitachi villa, Jijū answering "her lady" in person before departing with the aunt (Washburn NC pp. 306–307).
d.evidence: Heard by Suetsumuhana at the Hitachi villa, the two together until Jijū "left in a daze" (Washburn NC pp. 306–307); co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: within her own villa — "she had the edges of the eaves chambers wiped dry where rain had leaked in, and she had several rooms cleaned and tidied... She even composed this poem" (Washburn NC p. 308); the verse turns on those leaking eaves.
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly), at the Hitachi villa.

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: at the gate of the Hitachi villa — Genji has just confirmed "Is that the Hitachi villa?" (p. 308), and as Koremitsu sweeps the dew "in order for you to go in," "Genji murmured a poem to himself" (Washburn NC p. 310); the verse names the weed-choked "path."
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly), at the Hitachi villa gate.

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: inside the Princess's chambers at the Hitachi villa, which "Genji entered" (p. 310) and where "the moonlight flooded in, brightly illuminating the room all around them" (p. 311); prompted by the wisteria-pine in her garden he recites the verse (Washburn NC p. 311).
d.evidence: Heard by Suetsumuhana in her chambers at the Hitachi villa, present behind her inner curtains as Genji speaks (Washburn NC pp. 310–311); co-present.

c.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
r.name: The Hitachi villa (Suetsumuhana's ruined estate)
e.evidence: Location: in her chambers at the Hitachi villa, where Genji sat by her curtains and "the moonlight flooded in" upon the room (Washburn NC p. 311); there "the Princess replied" to him (Washburn NC p. 311).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji, present in her chambers at the Hitachi villa (Washburn NC pp. 310–311); co-present.

c.name: The Ōsaka Barrier (the gatehouse at Ōsaka Pass)
r.name: The Ōsaka Barrier (the gatehouse at Ōsaka Pass)
e.evidence: Location: in her carriage at the Ōsaka barrier — her party "pulled their carriages up the slope just below the barrier gate" (Washburn NC p. 314); Genji's message reaches her there ("I came to meet you at the barrier gate today," p. 314), and the verse turns on "this gate"/"this slope" (Washburn NC p. 315).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly), at the Ōsaka barrier.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The former Hitachi Deputy's residence (in the capital)
e.evidence: No locating text: back from Ishiyama, Genji simply "summoned the lady's younger brother again and gave him a letter to deliver" (Washburn NC p. 315), with no indication of where he was. Composition location inferred from context — his Nijō villa.
d.evidence: No locating text: her brother "took it to his sister" and she "couldn't resist replying" (Washburn NC p. 316), but her whereabouts are not stated. Receipt location inferred from context — her husband's (the former Hitachi Deputy's) residence in the capital.

c.name: The former Hitachi Deputy's residence (in the capital)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: No locating text: pressed by her brother, "she couldn't resist replying" (Washburn NC p. 316), with no indication of where she sat. Composition location inferred from context — her husband's residence in the capital.
d.evidence: No locating text: the reply is carried back to Genji, of whom only a general note follows — he "would disturb and disconcert her heart by sending letters on various occasions" (Washburn NC p. 316) — with no located reaction. Receipt location inferred from context — his Nijō villa.

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)
e.evidence: No text places Suzaku at the first dispatch; the gifts are merely "sent to her on the day that she began serving Emperor Reizei" (Washburn NC p. 317). Composition location inferred from context — the Retired Emperor's residence.
d.evidence: Location: the comb box reaches "the young lady's chambers" — "Genji happened to be in the young lady's chambers when the gifts arrived" (Washburn NC p. 317). Her chambers are the Umetsubo (named at p. 319).

c.name: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: Location: written in that same scene, in "the young lady's chambers" where the gifts had just arrived (Washburn NC p. 317), as her attendants pressed her for a reply (Washburn NC p. 318).
d.evidence: Location: Suzaku reads it at his own residence — "Genji called on him just after he had read the poem" (Washburn NC p. 319), i.e., Genji goes to him there.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Location: "Genji had chests and cabinets containing old and new illustrations at his Nijō villa brought out and opened, and together with Murasaki he selected and sorted various illustrations" (Washburn NC p. 321) — the setting of the exchange.
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at his Nijō villa, where the two were sorting illustrations together (Washburn NC p. 321); co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: Location: the same Nijō scene — the illustrations were "at his Nijō villa brought out and opened," sorted by Genji "together with Murasaki" (Washburn NC p. 321), where he answers her.
d.evidence: Heard by Murasaki at Nijō, the pair viewing the Suma sketches together (Washburn NC p. 321); co-present.

c.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
r.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
e.evidence: Location: "Fujitsubo also happened to be in residence at the palace at that time," and there she "divided them into two sides, the Left and the Right"; Heinaishi spoke for the Left (Washburn NC p. 322).
d.evidence: Recited before Fujitsubo at the palace, the opposing side (Daini) present (Washburn NC p. 322); co-present.

c.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
r.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
e.evidence: Location: the same debate, with Fujitsubo "in residence at the palace" presiding over the two sides (Washburn NC p. 322), where "Daini countered for the Right."
d.evidence: Recited before the presiding Fujitsubo at the palace, the Left also present (Washburn NC p. 322); co-present.

c.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
r.name: The palace (the ladies' picture debate before Fujitsubo)
e.evidence: Location: the same palace debate — Fujitsubo, "in residence at the palace" and presiding (Washburn NC p. 322), "interceded" with this verse (Washburn NC pp. 322–323).
d.evidence: Recited at the palace to Daini and the assembled ladies of both sides (Washburn NC pp. 322–323); co-present.

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)
e.evidence: Location: dispatched from Suzaku's residence — "A Captain of the Left Guard who was serving at the Retired Emperor's residence delivered the box and recited Suzaku's message" (Washburn NC p. 323).
d.evidence: Location: sent as a present "to Umetsubo" (Washburn NC p. 323) — to Akikonomu's chambers, where the box is delivered and she receives it (Washburn NC pp. 323–324).

c.name: The Umetsubo (Akikonomu's chambers, the plum courtyard)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: Location: composed at the Umetsubo, where Suzaku's collection had just been presented "to Umetsubo" (Washburn NC p. 323); she attaches the verse to a comb-piece and "had it taken to Suzaku" (Washburn NC p. 324).
d.evidence: Location: carried to Suzaku's residence — "had it taken to Suzaku" (Washburn NC p. 324), where he "read her reply poem" (Washburn NC p. 324).

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: null
d.evidence: null

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: null
d.evidence: null

c.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
r.name: The Akashi villa (Genji's seaside residence)
e.evidence: null
d.evidence: null

c.name: The boat departing Akashi
r.name: The boat departing Akashi
e.evidence: On the boat leaving Akashi — "they set sail" at the Hour of the Dragon, and the nun, "returning to the capital after living here for so many years," "wept" and composed the verse aboard (Washburn NC pp. 332–333).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady aboard the departing boat; co-present.

c.name: The boat departing Akashi
r.name: The boat departing Akashi
e.evidence: On the same departing boat, "The Akashi lady composed this" — the verse on "this drifting bark of sorrow" (Washburn NC p. 333).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Nun aboard the departing boat; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "the nun, who was lying down in a melancholy mood, arose as she heard" the wind in the pines and composed the verse (Washburn NC p. 334).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "Her daughter replied" to the nun in person (Washburn NC p. 334).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Nun at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, Genji "moved over to the nun's curtains and spoke to her"; as they "conversed about the past" and the cleared garden stream sounded, the nun composed the verse (Washburn NC pp. 335–336).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Ōi villa, present at the nun's curtains; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, at the nun's curtains, "He replied" in person, taking up her image of the garden stream (Washburn NC p. 336).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Nun at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: Back at the Ōi villa "while the moon was still bright," the Akashi Lady brings out the memento koto and Genji, playing it, composed the verse (Washburn NC p. 336).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "She replied" to Genji in person as they shared the koto (Washburn NC pp. 336–337).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Seiryōden
r.name: Genji's Katsura villa
e.evidence: At the palace, "where musical diversions were being enjoyed," the young Emperor Reizei, "Hearing that his Palace Minister was staying at Katsura... dispatched the men with a letter" (Washburn NC p. 339).
d.evidence: At Genji's Katsura villa, the messengers arrive and "enjoyed the music at the villa," and Genji "expressed his regrets for not attending the palace" (Washburn NC p. 339).

c.name: Genji's Katsura villa
r.name: The Seiryōden
e.evidence: At the Katsura villa, as "the Controller had to hurry back to the palace, Genji draped a set of robes over his left shoulder, and composed the following" (Washburn NC p. 339).
d.evidence: Carried back toward the palace by the Controller, who "had to hurry back" (Washburn NC p. 339). Receipt location inferred from context — Reizei at the palace; no reaction at receipt is shown.

c.name: Genji's Katsura villa
r.name: Genji's Katsura villa
e.evidence: At the Katsura villa, in the moonlit banquet, recalling "the moon over Awaji Island," "He composed another verse" aloud (Washburn NC p. 340).
d.evidence: Heard by the Middle Captain at the Katsura banquet, present; co-present.

c.name: Genji's Katsura villa
r.name: Genji's Katsura villa
e.evidence: At the Katsura banquet, "The Captain who rode with him in the carriage composed a response" aloud (Washburn NC p. 340).
d.evidence: Heard by the company at the Katsura banquet, including the Major Controller of the Left; co-present.

c.name: Genji's Katsura villa
r.name: Genji's Katsura villa
e.evidence: At the Katsura banquet, "The Major Controller of the Left... composed yet another response" aloud (Washburn NC p. 340).
d.evidence: Heard by the company at the Katsura banquet, including the Middle Captain; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, gazing from the veranda at the ice on the garden pond as snow fell, the Akashi Lady sighed and recited the verse to the nurse (Washburn NC p. 344).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Princess's Nurse at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "the nurse broke down and composed the following to console her lady" (Washburn NC p. 344).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, the Akashi Lady "took the little Princess in her arms and brought her out to where the carriages had been pulled up," then recited the verse (Washburn NC p. 345).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Ōi villa, present (he had come to take the child); co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "Genji found her anguish perfectly understandable" and answered her in person with the Takekuma-pine verse (Washburn NC p. 345).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, Murasaki, "waiting at the doorway to the corridor" as Genji left, "had composed a verse, which her attendant Chūjō recited to Genji" (Washburn NC p. 347).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at Nijō, present (taking his leave); co-present (relayed in the same room by Chūjō).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, "Chūjō spoke in such a lovely, intimate manner that Genji flashed a lustrous smile and replied" (Washburn NC p. 348).
d.evidence: Heard by Murasaki at Nijō, present at the corridor doorway; co-present (via Chūjō).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, Genji "hid away in the meditation pavilion at his residence, where he spent the day crying"; facing the setting sun and the thin gray clouds, he composed the verse (Washburn NC p. 352).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly) at the Nijō meditation pavilion — "With no one around to hear his verse, his words were spoken in vain" (Washburn NC p. 352).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, where Akikonomu had "withdrew from the palace and went to stay," Genji "went straight in behind her blinds. A single curtain separated them"; finding her reply irresistible, "he could not restrain himself" and recited the verse (Washburn NC pp. 356–357).
d.evidence: Heard by Akikonomu at Nijō, present behind her curtain; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, as the anglers' bonfires and fireflies flickered over the garden stream and Genji spoke, "The lady replied with a verse" (Washburn NC pp. 359–360).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
r.name: The Ōi villa (the Akashi Lady's residence near Katsura)
e.evidence: At the Ōi villa, "His response expressed his own complaint to her in turn" by the bonfires (Washburn NC p. 360).
d.evidence: Heard by the Akashi Lady at the Ōi villa, present; co-present.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, Genji, "shown into the outer chamber on the south side of the Princess's quarters" and speaking through her intermediary, recited the verse (Washburn NC p. 362).
d.evidence: Heard by Asagao at the Momozono estate, present behind her blinds (relayed by her intermediary); co-present.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, "she replied with a verse" through her intermediary (Washburn NC p. 362).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Momozono estate, present at her blinds; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At his Nijō villa the next morning, Genji "had the lattice shutters raised early in the morning and stared out at the mist-shrouded garden," then "had an attendant pick" bellflowers and "sent them to Princess Asagao" (Washburn NC p. 363).
d.evidence: Received by Asagao at the Momozono estate, where her women "brought in her inkstone" and she composed her reply (Washburn NC p. 363).

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, pressed by her women that a reply was due, Asagao wrote the bellflower verse on bluish-gray paper (Washburn NC p. 363).
d.evidence: Received by Genji at Nijō — "why did he keep staring at it, and find it hard to put aside?... enchanted by the soft color of her fluid brushstrokes" (Washburn NC p. 363).

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate's west gate, as the rusted lock "won't budge" and he reflects on the ruin since Prince Shikibu's death, Genji composed the verse (Washburn NC p. 366).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly) at the Momozono estate gate.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, in the Fifth Princess's quarters, the old Naishi "in a youthful mood... composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 367).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Momozono estate, present near her curtain; co-present.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, Genji answered Naishi in person, capping her verse (Washburn NC p. 367).
d.evidence: Heard by Gen no Naishi at the Momozono estate, present; co-present.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, late at night at Asagao's blinds, "Delicately brushing tears from his eyes, he composed the following" (Washburn NC p. 367).
d.evidence: Heard by Asagao at the Momozono estate, present behind her blinds; co-present.

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: At the Momozono estate, "Asagao replied to Genji" through her intermediary (Washburn NC p. 368).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Momozono estate, present at her blinds; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, as "the moon was glowing brilliantly" over the snow, "Murasaki composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 372).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at Nijō, present and gazing at her; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, "that symbol of marital fidelity prompted Genji's reply" (Washburn NC p. 372).
d.evidence: Heard by Murasaki at Nijō, present; co-present.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, startled awake from the vision of Fujitsubo, tears soaking his robes, "He murmured" the verse (Washburn NC p. 372).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (murmured inwardly) at Nijō, Murasaki lying beside him.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At Nijō, having risen early to commission sutra readings and invoke Amida, "Such, it has been reported, were his thoughts and feelings" as he framed the verse (Washburn NC pp. 372–373).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly) at Nijō.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Princess Asagao's residence
e.evidence: No locating text places Genji at composition; inferred from context — his Nijō villa (his established residence). "A message from Genji arrived inquiring after Asagao," with the verse, on purple paper attached to wisteria (Washburn NC p. 373).
d.evidence: Received by Asagao at her Momozono residence, where she mourned her father; "This particular day called forth deep emotions for the Princess, and so she sent this reply" (Washburn NC p. 373).

c.name: Princess Asagao's residence
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At her Momozono residence, moved by the day, Asagao "sent this reply," adding "All things must pass" (Washburn NC p. 373).
d.evidence: No locating text places Genji at receipt; inferred from context — his Nijō villa. "As usual, Genji could not stop poring over the letter" (Washburn NC p. 373).

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At the Sanjō residence, leaning against the locked sliding door to Kumoi no Kari's quarters at night, "The young man composed a verse" (Washburn NC p. 387).
d.evidence: Heard at the Sanjō residence by Kumoi no Kari, just beyond the door (she had whispered the line that names her); co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At the Sanjō residence, after a nurse disparaged his sixth rank, Yūgiri said "Just listen to her" and recited the verse (Washburn NC p. 391).
d.evidence: Heard by Kumoi no Kari at the Sanjō residence, present beside him; co-present.

c.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
r.name: The Sanjō estate (Princess Ōmiya's)
e.evidence: At the Sanjō residence, "Kumoinokari replied"; "She just finished her poem when her father entered" (Washburn NC p. 391).
d.evidence: Heard by Yūgiri at the Sanjō residence, present; co-present.

c.name: En route from the Sanjō residence to Nijō (dawn)
r.name: En route from the Sanjō residence to Nijō (dawn)
e.evidence: Leaving the Sanjō residence, Yūgiri "set off very early through the white frost of dawn to return to his rooms... at his father's Nijō villa"; "On his way back... The sky was still dark" as he framed the verse (Washburn NC p. 391).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly) on the dawn road home.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At the Nijō villa, having slipped "into the west hall," Yūgiri "rustled the hems of his robes to alert her to his presence" and recited the verse to the maiden behind the screens (Washburn NC p. 393).
d.evidence: Heard by Koremitsu's daughter (the Gosechi dancer) at the Nijō villa, present behind the screens; co-present.

c.name: The Shishinden
r.name: Indeterminate
e.evidence: At the palace during the Gosechi Festival, "Genji was in attendance, and as he looked at the dancers he was reminded of... his own lady of the Gosechi dance," prompting him to "write a letter to her on the evening of the Day of the Dragon" (Washburn NC p. 394).
d.evidence: No locating text — the Gosechi lady's whereabouts at receipt are not given (indeterminate).

c.name: Indeterminate
r.name: The Shishinden
e.evidence: No locating text — the Gosechi lady's whereabouts at composition are not given (indeterminate). She "had chosen paper appropriate to the season for her reply" (Washburn NC p. 394).
d.evidence: Received by Genji — inferred at the palace during the festival; "As he perused her note, Genji found her calligraphy enchanting" (Washburn NC p. 395).

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Koremitsu's residence
e.evidence: At the Nijō villa, Yūgiri wrote the verse on thin green paper matching her Gosechi garland and "handed the page a letter" to carry to his sister (Washburn NC pp. 395–396).
d.evidence: Received at Koremitsu's residence: "Koremitsu's daughter was enchanted by the note," and Koremitsu in turn read it there (Washburn NC pp. 395–396).

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: At the Suzaku Palace, during Emperor Reizei's progress, "When the dance was finished, Genji offered a winecup to Suzaku" with the verse (Washburn NC pp. 398–399).
d.evidence: Heard by Retired Emperor Suzaku at his palace, present; co-present.

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: At the Suzaku Palace, "Suzaku replied" to Genji's cup-verse (Washburn NC p. 399).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Suzaku Palace, present; co-present.

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: At the Suzaku Palace, "Prince Sochinomiya, who was now Minister of War, offered His Majesty a cup and... wittily turned them into a poem of praise" (Washburn NC p. 399).
d.evidence: Heard by Emperor Reizei at the Suzaku Palace, present; co-present.

c.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
r.name: The Suzaku Palace (Retired Emperor Suzaku's residence)
e.evidence: At the Suzaku Palace, "Taking the cup, Reizei replied" (Washburn NC p. 399).
d.evidence: Heard by Prince Hotaru at the Suzaku Palace, present; co-present.

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, from her autumn (southwest) quadrant, Akikonomu "mixed various flowers and leaves in the lid of a box and sent them to Murasaki's quarters" by a page girl, with the verse (Washburn NC p. 403).
d.evidence: Received by Murasaki in her spring (southeast) quadrant at the Rokujō estate; her "younger ladies-in-waiting... received the page" (Washburn NC p. 403).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, Murasaki built a miniature landscape — moss, rocks, a five-needle pine — in the box lid and "sent it with a poem attached to a branch of five-needle pine" (Washburn NC p. 403).
d.evidence: Received by Akikonomu in her autumn quadrant at the Rokujō estate; she "marveled at the ingenious craftsmanship" (Washburn NC p. 403).

c.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
r.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
e.evidence: Aboard the boat carrying the party to Kyūshū — "Off the bay at Ōshima" (named in the verse) — the daughters "composed their respective poems in response to their moods" (Washburn NC p. 406).
d.evidence: Heard aboard ship by Ateki, present; co-present on the voyage.

c.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
r.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
e.evidence: Aboard ship to Kyūshū, Ateki composed her verse "in response to" her sister's (Washburn NC p. 406).
d.evidence: Heard aboard ship by the elder daughter, present; co-present on the voyage.

c.name: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)
r.name: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)
e.evidence: At the Hizen household, "Taifu no Gen wanted to compose a poem as he was leaving" and recited it before the nurse (Washburn NC pp. 409–410).
d.evidence: Heard by the wet nurse at the Hizen household, present; co-present.

c.name: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)
r.name: The household in Hizen Province (Tamakazura's Kyūshū home)
e.evidence: At the Hizen household, the nurse "finally came up with" the reply verse, voice quavering (Washburn NC p. 410).
d.evidence: Heard by Taifu no Gen at the Hizen household — he "came barging back in" at her words; co-present.

c.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
r.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
e.evidence: Aboard the boat fleeing Kyūshū, "when their boat passed the beach in front of the Matsuura Shrine," Ateki looked back and composed the verse (Washburn NC p. 411).
d.evidence: Heard aboard ship by Tamakazura, present; co-present on the flight.

c.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
r.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
e.evidence: Aboard ship fleeing Kyūshū, "Her young mistress replied" with the verse, then "collapsed and lay prostrate" (Washburn NC p. 411).
d.evidence: Heard aboard ship by Ateki, present; co-present on the flight.

c.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
r.name: At sea, fleeing Kyūshū for the capital
e.evidence: Aboard ship off Hibiki no Nada (Harima coast), fearing the suitor's pursuit, "The nurse composed a poem" (Washburn NC p. 412).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (composed inwardly) aboard ship.

c.name: Hasedera (the Hatsuse Kannon temple)
r.name: Hasedera (the Hatsuse Kannon temple)
e.evidence: At Hasedera, as the party "could look down on the crowd of pilgrims... The Hatsuse River was flowing before them," "Ukon composed the following" (Washburn NC p. 419).
d.evidence: Heard by Tamakazura at Hasedera, present beside her; co-present.

c.name: Hasedera (the Hatsuse Kannon temple)
r.name: Hasedera (the Hatsuse Kannon temple)
e.evidence: At Hasedera, "The young lady replied"; "Upon composing her verse, she broke down and wept" (Washburn NC p. 419).
d.evidence: Heard by Ukon at Hasedera, present; co-present.

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Kujō lodging (Tamakazura's residence in the capital)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, cautious after his disappointment with Princess Safflower, Genji "sent a letter to the young lady" in a sober, fatherly tone, appending the verse (Washburn NC p. 422).
d.evidence: Received at the Kujō lodging, where "Ukon took Genji's letter and delivered it herself"; the young lady read it "feeling hesitant" (Washburn NC p. 422).

c.name: The Kujō lodging (Tamakazura's residence in the capital)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Kujō lodging, pressed by the nurse, the young lady "took out a piece of richly scented Chinese paper and wrote her reply poem" (Washburn NC pp. 422–423).
d.evidence: Received by Genji at the Rokujō estate — "Genji was relieved to see that it was not at all disappointing" (Washburn NC p. 423).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, talking with Murasaki, Genji "pulled an inkstone over and, as if practicing his calligraphy, scribbled the following" (Washburn NC p. 425).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee; "'My lovely garland... Tamakazura...,' he murmured to himself" at Rokujō.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the annex of the Nijō villa, where "his Princess Safflower" still lived, she wrote her reply on yellowed Michinokuni paper (Washburn NC pp. 427–428).
d.evidence: Received by Genji at the Rokujō estate — he "studied the letter with a wry smile" and would not put it down (Washburn NC p. 428).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, prodded by Murasaki that a reply was due, "Genji wrote back" in a casual tone (Washburn NC p. 429).
d.evidence: Sent to Suetsumuhana at the Nijō annex; inferred from context — her residence (no reaction shown; the chapter closes on Genji's reply).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, in Murasaki's spring quarter, Genji recited the seasonal greetings and "offered her the following" (Washburn NC p. 430).
d.evidence: Heard by Murasaki at the Rokujō estate, present; co-present.

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, "Murasaki replied" (Washburn NC p. 431).
d.evidence: Heard by Genji at the Rokujō estate, present; co-present.

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, from her own quarter, the Akashi lady "had sent over fruit and flowers... a remarkably well-crafted mechanical warbler" with the verse (Washburn NC p. 431).
d.evidence: Received in Murasaki's quarter at the Rokujō estate, where the Akashi Princess was being raised and Genji was visiting (Washburn NC p. 431).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, "He ordered an inkstone brought out and had her write" the reply herself (Washburn NC p. 432).
d.evidence: Received by the Akashi lady in her winter quarter at the Rokujō estate, where she "read it over and over" (Washburn NC p. 433).

c.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
r.name: The Rokujō estate (Genji's mansion)
e.evidence: At the Rokujō estate, in the Akashi lady's quarter, "as she read it over and over, she had jotted down" verses, among them this one (Washburn NC p. 433).
d.evidence: Speaker = addressee (jotted privately) in the Akashi lady's quarter at Rokujō.

c.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
r.name: Nijō (Genji's villa)
